meta.title: Axure Cloud 3.0: Leading as PM and Product Designer, discovery to design to launch
meta.oneLiner: Led Axure Cloud’s major refresh end-to-end as PM and Product Designer: design system and UI refresh in Figma, and seven shipped features, one narrative.

overview.companyContext: Axure Cloud is the collaboration and hosting platform for Axure RP prototypes. It's used by product managers, UX designers, and business analysts to share prototypes, specs, and design files. The platform had been built on a legacy, multi-page architecture and an older front-end stack; it had accumulated UI debt and performance issues as usage scaled. A major driver for the refresh was to move Cloud to a modern SPA (single-page application) built with Vue 3—and to prepare that experience for the upcoming launch of Axure Whiteboards and Documents. I led this effort in a dual role as both Product Manager and Product Designer—owning discovery and prioritization, the design system and UI refresh in Figma, and the sequencing of the architecture migration and release from research through to launch.
overview.mandate: Deliver a major update that rewrites the front end as a Vue 3 SPA, modernizes the visual design, and improves speed and responsiveness (especially in-app navigation). Ship a set of features that make files, workspaces, and settings easier to find and manage—without disrupting existing projects or integrations.
constraints: No breaking changes to existing links or embeds in this release
Design refresh had to feel cohesive across Cloud and RP sign-in flows
Target: ship before end of 2024 to align with annual planning and renewals

myContributions.title: PM: Discovery and prioritization
myContributions.body: As Product Manager, led user research and internal feedback sessions to define the design refresh scope and prioritize the seven improvements (quick search, recent files, comments, workspaces, activity feed, org moves, centralized settings).

myContributions.title: Product Designer: Design system and UI refresh
myContributions.body: As Product Designer, built the design system and refreshed the UI in Figma—owning visual direction, component specs, and the handoff to engineering. No separate design lead meant priorities and pixels stayed in lockstep.

myContributions.title: Dual role: Sequencing and launch
myContributions.body: Wearing both hats, I sequenced the SPA migration (Vue 2 → Vue 3), design refresh, and feature work into a single shippable release and coordinated the Dec 10 launch, changelog, and blog post so one narrative reached customers and prospects.

problem.userProblem: Users found Axure Cloud’s navigation and layout dated and slow. Every link or tab often triggered a full page reload, so the app felt sluggish; locating recent files, switching workspaces, and managing settings required too many clicks. Comment discovery and activity visibility were limited.
problem.businessProblem: Support and sales were fielding feedback about speed and “finding things.” Retention and NPS had room to improve, and the product needed to feel modern to compete with other collaboration tools.
problem.whyNow: The planned release of Axure Whiteboards and Documents required a Cloud experience that could serve as a modern entry point for both new products. We had already planned moving off the legacy multi-page stack to a Vue 3 SPA for performance and maintainability; leadership approved scope to pair that migration with a design refresh and a focused set of workflow improvements so one release could move the needle on both perception and daily use—and be ready for the Whiteboards and Documents launch.

workflowImage.caption: Pre-refresh Axure Cloud: navigation and layout before the update.

process.researchApproach: We combined support themes, in-app feedback, and sessions with power users and new adopters to map where the experience felt slow or confusing. We also ran internal dogfooding to prioritize which improvements would have the highest impact for the engineering and design effort required. Being both PM and Product Designer meant I could translate research into scope and then into concrete UI direction without a handoff—what we learned in discovery directly shaped what I designed in Figma, and trade-offs between “ship more” and “polish more” were made in one place.

technoFunctional: The front end had to be migrated from the legacy multi-page stack to a Vue 3 SPA—rebuilding routing, state, and key flows without breaking existing links.
We upgraded from Vue 2 to Vue 3 and had to scope the migration so we could ship on schedule.
Settings and workspace logic were spread across multiple surfaces; we had to consolidate without breaking existing org and permission models.
experiential: Users didn’t have a single way to jump to recent work or search across files and workspaces; we needed quick search and recent files as first-class entry points.
Comment and activity context were hard to find; we needed search, sort, and a revamped activity feed to surface what mattered.

keyDecisions.decision: Rewrite the app as a Vue 3 SPA and ship it in one major release with the design refresh and seven improvements.
keyDecisions.rationale: Moving to a SPA eliminated full page reloads and made in-app navigation fast. Shipping SPA, design refresh, and features together created a clear “before and after” story and one cohesive update for users and sales.

keyDecisions.decision: Upgrade from Vue 2 to Vue 3 as part of the SPA rewrite.
keyDecisions.rationale: Vue 3’s composition API, better performance, and ecosystem alignment made it the right base for the new front end; doing it in one migration avoided maintaining two stacks.

keyDecisions.decision: Quick search (Cmd+K / Ctrl+K) and recent files in the sidebar as anchor features.
keyDecisions.rationale: Research showed that “finding my file” and “getting back to recent work” were top friction points; keyboard search and recent files addressed both with minimal new UI.

keyDecisions.decision: One person owning both PM and Product Design for the refresh.
keyDecisions.rationale: Keeping discovery, prioritization, and visual design in a single role reduced coordination overhead and ensured that what we prioritized was exactly what got designed—no “someone else will make it look good” gap. The trade-off was scope discipline; we had to be strict about the seven improvements so the dual role stayed manageable.

solution.overview: We rewrote Axure Cloud as a Vue 3 single-page application and shipped a design refresh across the product. In-app navigation no longer triggers full page reloads—switching between files, workspaces, and settings is fast. We delivered seven improvements: quick search, recent files in the sidebar, enhanced comments (search and sort), Browse Workspaces, revamped activity feed, move workspaces between organizations, and centralized settings.

baselineImages.caption: Before: previous Cloud layout and navigation.
After: refreshed design with quick search and recent files.

features.title: Quick search and recent files
features.problem: Users had no fast way to find files, folders, or workspaces or to return to recent work.
features.solution: Quick search (Cmd+K on Mac, Ctrl+K on Windows) to locate files, folders, and workspaces; recent files added to the sidebar for quick access.

after.caption: After: quick search (Cmd+K) and recent files

features.title: Enhanced comments and activity
features.problem: Comments and activity were hard to search, sort, or scan for what mattered.
features.solution: Comment functionality enhanced with search and sort; revamped activity feed to highlight important events.

features.title: Workspaces and settings
features.problem: Discovering and joining workspaces was unclear; settings were scattered; moving workspaces between organizations wasn’t supported.
features.solution: Browse Workspaces to find and join organization workspaces; ability to move workspaces between organizations; centralized settings on a single page.

stats.label: In-app page switches with SPA architecture
NPS improvement in first quarter post-launch
Faster time to find file in search scenarios

quantitative: Moving to a Vue 3 SPA removed full page reloads on navigation; in-app page switches are sub-100ms and we saw meaningful gains in core Web Vitals.
Quick search and recent files drove higher reuse of the sidebar and fewer “where did I put that?” support questions in the first weeks post-launch.
Centralized settings reduced support tickets related to “where do I change X?” and simplified onboarding for new admins.
qualitative: Internal and beta users reported that the product “feels like a real app now” and “easier to get around” with instant navigation instead of full reloads.
Sales and customer success used the changelog and blog post to explain the SPA and design update clearly; feedback from enterprise accounts was positive.
results.retrospective: Moving to a Vue 3 SPA and combining it with the design refresh and seven improvements in one release was the right call for impact and narrative. The SPA architecture made the product feel modern and fast; keeping existing links unchanged avoided risk and let us focus adoption messaging on the new experience. Operating as both PM and Product Designer was a strength: no handoff between “what we’re building” and “how it looks” meant faster iteration and a single story for stakeholders. The main lesson was scope discipline—the dual role only works when the mandate is clear and bounded; we had to say no to extra features so both roles could be done well. Earlier alignment with docs and support on the centralized settings map would have made launch week smoother.
